basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Kāfirūn — “The Disbelievers” — is among the earliest Meccan sūras. On the historical plane, it answers a proposal made to the Prophet by the leaders of Quraysh: that he worship their idols for a month and they would worship his God for a year, blending the religions. The Prophet refused, and the sūra came down. But Qushayrī, refusing to halt at the episode, gives a reading that turns the sūra from outside to within: what it refuses is less the idolatry of another than worship according to one's own choosing. True servitude, he writes, “is the abandoning of choice (ikhtiyār, اخْتِيَار); it appears as peace under the reversals of destiny, without breaking. It is the absence, in the heart, of any aversion — through whatever turn the Master makes us pass.” Thus “I do not worship what you worship” is no longer a refusal of idols only; it is the refusal to mix the servant's pure worship with anything that would come from his own choice. The sūra teaches, at depth, ʿubūdiyya — pure servitude.

versets.commentaire: al-kāfirūn (الكَافِرُون): those who cover. The root k-f-r first means “to veil, to cover” — the farmer who covers the seed under the earth is called kāfir. In its religious sense, it names the one who covers the truth he has received. The sūra opens with a direct address; and the spiritual address, in Sufi reading, extends to all that in us veils the received light — every thought, every habit, every reflex by which we keep on covering what we already know.

mots.def: kāfir: the one who covers, who veils the received truth. Root k-f-r: to cover.

versets.trad: I do not worship what you worship,
versets.commentaire: The verb ʿabada (عَبَدَ) means to serve, to worship; the root carries the sense of full submission — a ʿabd (عَبْد) is at once servant and worshipper. Qushayrī brings the statement to its ontological level: “Servitude (ʿubūdiyya) consists in standing by His command, in the manner He commanded, in the measure He commanded, at the time He commanded.” I do not worship what you worship says not only: not your idols. It says also: not in your manner, not in your measure, not in your time. Pure worship admits no admixture — not even of our own style.

mots.def: ʿabada: to worship, to serve in submission; imperfect, 1st pers. sing.

versets.trad: nor do you worship what I worship.
versets.commentaire: Here the sūra uses the active participle — ʿābidūn (عَابِدُون), “worshippers” —, which names not an isolated act but a lasting posture. What you are as worshippers cannot mingle with what I am. For Qushayrī it is also a word to every soul: what in you worships of one's own pleasure (according to taste, choice, mood) cannot mingle with what worships without choice (according to His pure designation). The two postures are foreign to one another.

mots.def: active participle of ʿabada: worshipper; in the plural, names a lasting posture.

versets.trad: And I shall not worship what you have worshipped,
versets.commentaire: The past enters the sūra: mā ʿabadtum, “what you have worshipped”. The servant's constancy crosses even the time that preceded him — he never worshipped and will never worship what is not God. Qushayrī, soberly, gives the key: “The truth of servitude lies in the abandoning of choice.” What changes when this abandoning is accomplished is not the present alone: it is everything in the past that still bore the trace of a self-choice. The verse sweeps that too.

mots.def: active participle of ʿabada: worshipper; indefinite singular.
ʿabada: to worship; perfect, 2nd pers. masc. plur.: you have worshipped.

versets.trad: nor will you worship what I worship.
versets.commentaire: The sūra repeats v. 3 — a rare repetition in the Quran, and therefore charged. For the Sufis, what is said twice does not say the same thing: the first you do not worship what I worship bore on the present; this one bears on the future, in contrast to v. 4 which bore on the past. The sūra thus covers the whole of duration — past, present, future. Every tense of the soul is covered by the same separation — not as a fight, but as a sheer incompatibility of essence between the two orientations.

versets.trad: Unto you your religion, and unto me my religion.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī gives the key of the final verse: “Unto you your religion — that is: to you, the recompense of your religion. And to me, the recompense of mine.” The verse is not only a word of peace leaving each to his way; it is also the implicit announcement that each way bears its fruit. Dīn (دِين) means both religion and judgment: what one does, one reaps, and each soul will have the measure of its orient. The verse closes the sūra without violence, but without concession — not by lazy tolerance, but by inner firmness. It is ʿubūdiyya that speaks here: he who knows to whom he belongs knows also to whom he does not belong.

mots.def: preposition li- + suffix pronoun 2nd pers. masc. plur.: to you, for you.
dīn: religion, way, due order, judgment; with the plural suffix pronoun: your religion.
preposition li- + suffix pronoun 1st pers. sing.: to me, for me.
dīn: religion, way; the possessive suffix -ī is here elided in pronunciation.
